title: Example Document
excerpt:
Overview
This is an example document demonstrating the required frontmatter format for
Lorengine. Every markdown file in needs a field in its YAML
frontmatter — all other fields are optional but recommended.
Delete this file after setting up your fork and replace it with your own content.
Frontmatte
body:
Overview
This is an example document demonstrating the required frontmatter format for
Lorengine. Every markdown file in needs a field in its YAML
frontmatter — all other fields are optional but recommended.
Delete this file after setting up your fork and replace it with your own content.
Frontmatter Fields
- title (required): The display name of the document
- tags: A list of tags for categorisation and search filtering
- status: One of , , or 
- last-updated: The date this document was last modified (YYYY-MM-DD)
Writing Guidelines
Use and headings to structure your documents. These headings are
extracted by the build script for the table of contents and search index.
Markdown Features
Standard markdown is supported:
- Bold and italic text
- and fenced code blocks
- Links, images, and lists
- Tables and blockquotes
Tips
- Keep documents focused on a single topic
- Use descriptive titles and relevant tags
- Update the field when making changes
- Set to when the document is ready for reference

 because they share the same folder.

follows the sort setting (default: A–Z by title).

body:
Overview
This section contains example pages that demonstrate how the tree sidebar organises docs.
Pages placed directly inside a folder become children of the file named after that folder.
For example, and are siblings here because both live in
 and this file is named — matching the folder name.
Key rule
A file named the same as its containing folder becomes the section root for that folder.
Every other file in that folder is a direct child of the root.
Subsections
Subfolder roots (e.g. ) appear as independent top-level nodes in the
tree — they are not children of this page. The tree only tracks one level of folder nesting
per section root.

title: Simple Page
excerpt:
Overview
This is a flat leaf page — it has no children and lives directly under the Examples section.
In the tree sidebar it appears as a plain link under Examples, with no expand toggle.
When to use flat pages
Use flat pages for standalone topics that don't need sub-pages — reference entries,
one-o
body:
Overview
This is a flat leaf page — it has no children and lives directly under the Examples section.
In the tree sidebar it appears as a plain link under Examples, with no expand toggle.
When to use flat pages
Use flat pages for standalone topics that don't need sub-pages — reference entries,
one-off guides, or short factual docs that won't grow into a section.

body:
Overview
This page is the root of the folder. It appears as an independent top-level
node in the tree sidebar — not as a child of Examples — because section roots are always
promoted to the top level.
Its children (, ) appear nested beneath it when expanded.
When to use nested sections
Use a subfolder with a matching root file when a topic has enough sub-pages to warrant
grouping — typically three or more related pages that share a parent concept.

title: Getting Started
excerpt:
Welcome
Welcome to the wiki! This is a static site built with Lorengine, a self-hosted markdown-based knowledge base.
Adding Pages
Create markdown files in the folder with frontmatter:
Features
- Full-text search powered by Lunr.js

body:
Welcome
Welcome to the wiki! This is a static site built with Lorengine, a self-hosted markdown-based knowledge base.
Adding Pages
Create markdown files in the folder with frontmatter:
Features
- Full-text search powered by Lunr.js
- AI sidebar powered by the Claude API
- Multiple themes — Light, Dark, Sepia, High Contrast
- Tree navigation with folder hierarchy
- Backlinks — see which pages reference the current one
Search
Use the search page to find content across all pages instantly.
AI Assistant
Each page includes an AI assistant sidebar. Enter your Anthropic API key when prompted — it is never stored in the repository.

title: Markdown Elements Test
excerpt:
About This Page
This page tests every markdown element the wiki engine renders. It is a child of Getting Started and links to several sibling pages. Use it to verify that headings, inline styles, code blocks, tables, lists, blockquotes, and links all render correctly.
---
Text Formatting
Normal para
body:
About This Page
This page tests every markdown element the wiki engine renders. It is a child of Getting Started and links to several sibling pages. Use it to verify that headings, inline styles, code blocks, tables, lists, blockquotes, and links all render correctly.
---
Text Formatting
Normal paragraph text. Bold text stands out. Italic text provides emphasis. Bold and italic together for strong emphasis. ~~Strikethrough~~ marks deprecated content. renders in monospace.
You can combine styles: a bold sentence with an italic word inside. Or reference code inline: pass to enable debug output.
Line breaks within a paragraph are soft-wrapped. A blank line starts a new paragraph.
---
Headings
H3 — Section Level
Content under an H3 heading. The TOC on the right should list this as a sub-entry under the parent H2.
Another H3
Multiple H3s under the same H2 should all appear in the TOC.
H4 — Subsection
H4 headings provide a third level. Not all themes distinguish H4 visually from H3 — worth checking here.
---
Links
Internal Links
- Getting Started — parent page
- Examples — sibling section root
- Simple Page — child of Examples
- Another Page — sibling of Simple Page
- Scroll Test — long page for sidebar testing
External Links
- CommonMark Spec — markdown reference
- Lunr.js — search library used by this wiki
- marked.js — markdown renderer used by this wiki
Anchor Links
Jump to a heading on this page: Code Blocks, Tables, Images.
---
Lists
Unordered List
- First item
- Second item
 - Nested item A
 - Nested item B
 - Deeply nested
- Third item
- Fourth item with a longer description that wraps to the next line to test how the layout handles multi-line list items in narrower viewports.
Ordered List
1. First step
2. Second step
 1. Sub-step A
 2. Sub-step B
3. Third step
4. Fourth step
Mixed Nesting
1. Ordered parent
 - Unordered child
 - Another unordered child
2. Second ordered item
 - Nested bullet
 1. Numbered leaf
Task List
- [x] Completed task
- [x] Another completed task
- [ ] Pending task
- [ ] Another pending task
---
Code Blocks
Plain Block
Python
JavaScript
YAML Frontmatter Example
Shell
---
Blockquotes
> A single-line blockquote.
> A longer blockquote that spans multiple lines. The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. The rain in Spain falls mainly on the plain. Pack my box with five dozen liquor jugs.
> Blockquote with formatting. Italic text inside a blockquote. And too.
> Nested blockquotes:
>
> > This is a nested blockquote. It is indented one level further. Some markdown renderers support this; verify it renders correctly here.
---
Tables
Simple Table
| Column A | Column B | Column C |
|---|---|---|
| Row 1A | Row 1B | Row 1C |
| Row 2A | Row 2B | Row 2C |
| Row 3A | Row 3B | Row 3C |
Aligned Table
| Name | Type | Default | Description |
|:---|:---|:---:|---:|
| | string | — | Page title (required) |
| | list | | Tag list for filtering |
| | string | | One of draft, review, final |
| | int | | Lower = earlier in tree |
| | string | — | Parent page slug |
Wider Table
| Endpoint | Method | Auth | Request Body | Response | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| | GET | Optional | — | Page list | Filtered by tag |
| | GET | Optional | — | Page detail | Includes backlinks |
| | POST | Required | Page object | Created page | Slug auto-generated |
| | PUT | Required | Partial page | Updated page | Partial update OK |
| | DELETE | Required | — | 204 No Content | Irreversible |
---
Images
Image with Alt Text
!Placeholder image — 400×200
Image with Caption (via link wrapping)
A placeholder image used for testing
Figure: A test image. Click to open full size.
---
Horizontal Rules
Three ways to write a horizontal rule — they should all render identically:
---
___
---
Inline HTML
The wiki renders markdown via marked.js. Some inline HTML elements may pass through:
Ctrl + S — keyboard key styling.
Highlighted text using the HTML mark element.
Collapsible section (click to expand)
This content is hidden until the summary is clicked. This uses native HTML and elements, which are not standard markdown but may render in the wiki depending on DOMPurify's allow-list.
---
Parent and Child Relationships
This page sets in its frontmatter, which makes it a child of Getting Started in the tree sidebar. The backlinks section at the bottom of Getting Started should list this page once the build runs.
To see hierarchy in action:
- Parent of this page: Getting Started
- Sibling pages (also children of Getting Started): none currently — this is the only child
- Examples section (independent tree root): Examples → children: Simple Page, Another Page
The tree sidebar should show this page indented under Getting Started. The backlinks panel at the bottom of this page lists pages that link here.
---
Edge Cases
Long Unbroken String
This tests word-wrap for long tokens: — should wrap or scroll, not break the layout.
A URL without a link wrapper: https://www.example.com/very/long/path/that/goes/on/and/on/and/on?query=value&another=value2&third=value3
Empty Table Cells
| A | B | C |
|---|---|---|
| | filled | |
| filled | | filled |
| | | |
Escaped Characters
Literal asterisks: \not italic\ and \\not bold\\*. Literal backtick: \`. Literal backslash: \\. Literal brackets: \[not a link\].
Unicode
Greek: αβγδεζηθικλμνξοπρστυφχψω — Cyrillic: АБВГДЕЁЖЗИЙКЛМНОПРСТУФХЦЧШЩЪЫЬЭЮЯ — CJK: 日本語テスト中文简体한국어 — Emoji: 🎮 🗺️ ⚔️ 🏰 📜

title: Scroll Test — Long Page
headings: The Cost of Shared Mutable State
Documentation as Part of the Contract
Latency Numbers Every Engineer Should Know
The CAP Theorem and Its Limits
excerpt:
Introduction
This page exists purely to test sticky sidebar and TOC behaviour on long pages. It contains many headings and enough body content to require significant vertical scrolling. Scroll down and watch the sidebars track correctly — the tree sidebar on the left and the table of contents on the
body:
Introduction
This page exists purely to test sticky sidebar and TOC behaviour on long pages. It contains many headings and enough body content to require significant vertical scrolling. Scroll down and watch the sidebars track correctly — the tree sidebar on the left and the table of contents on the right should both remain anchored below the header and stop when their bottom reaches the viewport bottom.
Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.
---
Chapter One — The Beginning
The First Principle
Every complex system begins with a simple rule. The art is in finding which rule governs the rest. In software, this is often the invariant — the thing that must remain true regardless of what changes around it.
Consider a tree. Its structure is defined by a single recursive relationship: every node has exactly one parent, except the root which has none. From that single constraint emerges every property of the tree — traversal order, depth, balance, height.
The Second Principle
Complexity is not intrinsically bad. It becomes bad when it is accidental — when it arises not from the inherent difficulty of the problem but from choices made without care. Accidental complexity compounds. Each new layer of unnecessary abstraction adds weight without adding value.
The discipline of software is largely the discipline of recognising and refusing accidental complexity.
The Third Principle
Every abstraction leaks. No matter how clean the interface, the underlying implementation details eventually surface. The question is not whether your abstractions will leak, but when, and whether you will be ready for it when they do.
Good engineers design for the leak. They know their abstractions are approximations and keep the underlying reality close enough to reason about when the surface cracks.
---
Chapter Two — Data and State
The Nature of State
State is the memory of a system. Without state, every computation is pure and repeatable — given the same inputs, you always get the same outputs. With state, the same inputs may produce different outputs depending on history.
Most bugs live in state. The incorrect value, the stale cache, the partially committed transaction, the shared mutable reference — these are the fault lines where software cracks under pressure.
Immutability as a Design Choice
Immutability does not eliminate state. It transforms it. Instead of mutating existing values, you create new values. The history is preserved. Old states can be inspected, compared, restored.
This is why functional programming has gained ground in domains where correctness matters most: financial systems, compilers, distributed databases. The ability to reason about what was, not just what is, turns out to be invaluable.
The Cost of Shared Mutable State
When two parts of a system can both read and write the same piece of memory without coordination, you have shared mutable state. This is the source of data races, deadlocks, and a class of bugs that only appear in production under load.
The solutions are well-known: locks, semaphores, channels, actors, transactional memory. Each has trade-offs. None is free. The real question is how much of your system genuinely needs to share mutable state, and whether that sharing could be eliminated by a different design.
Caches and Their Discontents
There are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation, naming things, and off-by-one errors. The joke is old but the pain is fresh. Caches are state that is deliberately allowed to become stale. The entire correctness argument rests on the claim that staleness is acceptable — that the cost of a slightly wrong answer is lower than the cost of computing the right one.
That claim is often true. It is not always checked.
---
Chapter Three — Interfaces and Contracts
What an Interface Promises
An interface is a contract between a provider and a consumer. The provider promises to behave in a certain way. The consumer is entitled to rely on that behaviour. When the contract is violated — either by a provider that does not honour its promise, or by a consumer that depends on behaviour outside the contract — things break.
The hardest interfaces to maintain are those where the contract was never written down. The implementation becomes the specification by default, and every quirk becomes load-bearing.
The Principle of Least Surprise
Good interfaces behave the way users expect them to. This sounds obvious. It is surprisingly hard to achieve. It requires understanding who your users are, what mental models they bring, and where those models differ from the underlying reality.
The standard library of any language is a masterclass in this — both as a positive example and as a collection of cautionary tales about decisions made early that could not be undone.
Versioning and Stability
Stability is not the same as immovability. A stable interface can evolve — but it does so in ways that preserve backwards compatibility. Adding is usually safe. Removing is not. Changing the semantics of existing behaviour is the most dangerous move of all, because it may break consumers who never expected the change and whose breakage will manifest in surprising ways far from the source.
Semantic versioning exists to communicate intent about compatibility. It is only as useful as the discipline of the team using it.
Documentation as Part of the Contract
An undocumented interface has an implicit contract: everything the implementation does is fair game for consumers to rely on. This makes refactoring expensive, testing difficult, and maintenance painful.
Documentation is not bureaucracy. It is the act of deciding, explicitly, what you are committing to — and what you are not. Writing the docs forces the question. The question is worth asking.
---
Chapter Four — Performance
Measure Before Optimising
The first rule of performance work is also the most frequently violated: measure before you optimise. Intuitions about performance are wrong often enough that acting on them without data is a gamble. The code you are certain is the bottleneck frequently is not.
Profile first. Find the actual hot path. Optimise that. Measure again to confirm the improvement. This loop is slower than just hacking at the thing that feels slow, and it produces better results.
The Hierarchy of Optimisations
Not all optimisations are equal. The largest gains usually come from algorithmic improvements — changing O(n²) to O(n log n) makes the code faster for all inputs, not just the ones you tested. Smaller gains come from data structure choices, memory access patterns, branch prediction, instruction-level parallelism.
The temptation is to skip straight to the micro-optimisations because they are tangible and satisfying. The discipline is to look for the algorithmic win first.
The Memory Hierarchy
Modern CPUs are fast. Memory is slow. The gap between processing speed and memory latency is one of the defining constraints of contemporary software performance, and most of what the CPU microarchitecture does — caches, prefetchers, out-of-order execution — is an attempt to paper over that gap.
Writing cache-friendly code means writing code that accesses memory in predictable, sequential patterns. Arrays beat linked lists for traversal. Struct-of-arrays can beat array-of-structs for processing. The difference is often surprising in magnitude.
Latency Numbers Every Engineer Should Know
Some numbers are worth internalising. L1 cache reference: ~1ns. L2 cache reference: ~4ns. L3 cache reference: ~10ns. Main memory reference: ~100ns. SSD random read: ~16µs. Network round trip within same datacenter: ~500µs. Cross-continental network round trip: ~150ms.
These numbers change with hardware generations but the ratios remain roughly stable. They give you a sense of what is cheap and what is expensive, and which optimisations are fighting the right battles.
---
Chapter Five — Reliability
Failure Is the Default
Distributed systems fail. Networks partition. Disks corrupt. Services crash. Processes run out of memory. Hardware fails. The question is not whether failure will occur but how your system responds when it does.
Reliability engineering is the discipline of building systems that degrade gracefully — that lose capability without losing correctness, that recover automatically where possible, and that fail visibly when they cannot.
The CAP Theorem and Its Limits
The CAP theorem says that a distributed system can guarantee at most two of three properties: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance. Since network partitions are unavoidable, the practical trade-off is between consistency and availability.
This framing has been critiqued as too coarse. The real space of trade-offs is richer than a triangle. But as a mental model for thinking about what your system prioritises under duress, it remains useful.
Retries, Idempotency, and Exactly-Once Delivery
When a network call fails, should you retry? Usually yes — but only if the operation is idempotent, meaning that calling it multiple times has the same effect as calling it once. Non-idempotent operations retried on failure can cause duplicate charges, duplicate records, or double-sends.
Exactly-once delivery is famously difficult to achieve. In practice, most systems offer at-least-once delivery with idempotency as the mechanism for handling duplicates. Designing your operations to be idempotent is therefore not optional — it is the price of operating at scale.
Observability
You cannot fix what you cannot see. Observability — the degree to which you can infer the internal state of a system from its external outputs — is not a luxury. It is the minimum requirement for operating a system you are accountable for.
Logs, metrics, and traces are the three pillars. Logs tell you what happened. Metrics tell you the shape of what is happening over time. Traces tell you how a single request moved through your system. Each answers questions the others cannot.
---
Chapter Six — Teams and Process
Conway's Law
Any organisation that designs a system will produce a design whose structure mirrors the communication structure of that organisation. This is Conway's Law, and it is more predictive than most engineering teams would like to admit.
The implication is that if you want to change the architecture, you may need to change the team structure. Or, working in the other direction: when you restructure teams, expect the architecture to drift to match.
The Cost of Coordination
Every handoff between people adds latency and the possibility of misunderstanding. The more people who must agree before a decision can be made, the slower the decision. This is not an argument against coordination — coordination is how you avoid expensive mistakes — but it is an argument for minimising unnecessary coordination, and for being deliberate about which decisions require it.
Small, autonomous teams with clear ownership and well-defined interfaces to the rest of the organisation tend to move faster and with less friction.
Code Review as Communication
Code review is often treated as a quality gate. It is also — and perhaps more importantly — a communication mechanism. It is how knowledge of the codebase spreads, how standards are maintained without a style guide, and how less experienced engineers learn by example.
The review comment that explains not just what to change but why is more valuable than the one that merely identifies the problem. The explanation becomes part of the shared understanding of the team.
Technical Debt as Investment
Technical debt is not inherently bad. Sometimes it is the right trade-off — shipping something imperfect now to learn from real usage before spending more on the right solution. The problem is when debt is taken without accounting for it, when interest payments accumulate silently, when the weight of past decisions makes every new change slow and expensive.
The discipline is in making debt visible, intentional, and serviced. Not zero debt — that may be impossible or undesirable — but debt that is understood and managed.
---
Chapter Seven — Security
Threat Modelling
Security work begins with a question: what are you protecting, from whom, and under what constraints? Without answers to these questions, security measures are guesswork — you may be building moats around the wrong castle.
Threat modelling is the practice of thinking systematically about adversaries, their capabilities, their motivations, and the attack surfaces available to them. It is not a one-time activity. It should happen when the system changes and when the threat landscape changes.
The Principle of Least Privilege
Every component of a system should have access to exactly what it needs to do its job, and no more. This limits the blast radius of a compromise — if an attacker gains control of a component, they inherit that component's permissions, not the permissions of the entire system.
Least privilege is easy to state and surprisingly hard to enforce consistently. It requires ongoing attention as systems evolve and permissions drift.
Defence in Depth
No single security control is sufficient. Defence in depth means layering controls so that the failure of any one layer does not lead to a complete breach. Perimeter defence and endpoint security and network segmentation and application-level controls and data encryption — each layer catches what the others miss.
The assumption underlying defence in depth is that some controls will fail. This is a realistic assumption.
Input Validation at the Boundary
Every piece of data that enters a system from outside its trust boundary is potentially hostile. Validate it. Sanitise it. Do not pass unvalidated input to interpreters — SQL engines, shell commands, HTML renderers — that will execute it as code.
The OWASP Top Ten has looked roughly the same for two decades because injection vulnerabilities keep being introduced. The fix is always the same: treat untrusted input as data, never as instructions.
---
Chapter Eight — The Long View
Software Decays
Software left unmaintained degrades. Not because the bits rot, but because the world around them changes. Dependencies release new versions. Operating systems change their APIs. Security vulnerabilities are discovered. Hardware evolves. The code that ran fine last year may not run fine next year without attention.
Maintenance is not the absence of change. It is the work of keeping a system relevant and correct as everything around it moves.
The Economics of Rewriting
The temptation to rewrite is perennial. The existing code is crufty and hard to understand; a fresh start would let you do it right. This is almost always more optimistic than warranted. The existing code embeds hard-won knowledge about edge cases, failure modes, and user behaviour. The new code will rediscover all of those lessons, and pay for the rediscovery.
Rewrites sometimes make sense. They make sense less often than engineers believe.
Knowing When to Stop
Not every problem needs to be solved. Not every feature needs to be built. Not every optimisation needs to be pursued to its logical conclusion. Knowing when the current solution is good enough — not perfect, but adequate for the actual need — is a skill as important as any other.
Perfectionism in engineering is expensive. The goal is not the perfect system. The goal is a system that solves the actual problem reliably, at a cost the organisation can afford, with a maintainability profile that does not make future work impossible.
Craft and Care
Good software requires care. Not just technical skill — though that is necessary — but genuine attention to whether the thing works, whether the people using it are served by it, whether the people maintaining it can understand it.
Craft is not ornamentation. It is the difference between code written to work and code written to last. The former may look the same from the outside. The inside tells the story.
---
Appendix A — Reference Tables
Common Time Complexities
| Operation | Complexity | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Hash table lookup | O(1) avg | Dictionary access |
| Binary search | O(log n) | Sorted array search |
| Linear scan | O(n) | Unsorted array search |
| Merge sort | O(n log n) | General-purpose sort |
| Naive string match | O(nm) | Substring search |
| Floyd-Warshall | O(n³) | All-pairs shortest path |
HTTP Status Codes
| Range | Meaning | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|
| 2xx | Success | 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content |
| 3xx | Redirect | 301 Moved, 302 Found, 304 Not Modified |
| 4xx | Client error | 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found |
| 5xx | Server error | 500 Internal Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Unavailable |
---
Appendix B — Further Reading
Books Worth Reading
The literature on software engineering is vast and uneven. A short list of genuinely useful texts: The Pragmatic Programmer for breadth; Designing Data-Intensive Applications for distributed systems; Working Effectively with Legacy Code for the reality most engineers spend their time in; A Philosophy of Software Design for thinking about complexity; Site Reliability Engineering for operating systems at scale.
Staying Current
The field moves fast in some directions and slowly in others. Fundamentals — algorithms, data structures, concurrency, networking — change little. Fashions in languages, frameworks, and tooling change constantly. Spending most of your learning time on fundamentals and a fraction on current practice is usually the right balance.
The Best Way to Learn
Write code. Read code written by people better than you. Have your code reviewed. Review others' code. Repeat. There is no substitute for the feedback loop of building things, seeing where they break, and understanding why.
